label: Vector
definition: An ordered list of numbers that represents a point or a direction in space, and the fundamental object that machine learning uses to encode data, features, and model parameters.

body.text: A vector is an ordered list of numbers, written as a column or a row, that you can think of in two complementary ways: as a point at some location in space, or as an arrow pointing from the origin to that location. A three-number vector lives in three-dimensional space, and a vector with a thousand numbers lives in a thousand-dimensional space that you cannot picture but can still reason about with exactly the same rules. Each number is a coordinate, the amount of the vector along one independent direction.

Vectors matter because they are the universal container for data in machine learning. A house becomes a vector of its square footage, bedroom count, and price. A word becomes a vector of hundreds of learned numbers, an embedding, whose geometry captures meaning. The parameters of a neural network, the activations flowing through it, and the gradients that train it are all vectors. Once data is in vector form, the whole toolkit of linear algebra applies to it.

Two operations make a collection of vectors a usable space. You can add two vectors by adding their coordinates one position at a time, which corresponds to placing the arrows tip to tail. You can scale a vector by multiplying every coordinate by a single number, which stretches or shrinks the arrow and flips its direction if the number is negative. These two operations, addition and scaling, are the defining structure of a vector space, and almost everything else in linear algebra is built on them.

A vector also has a length and a direction. The length, formalized by a norm, measures how far the point sits from the origin. The direction is what remains when you divide a vector by its length, giving a unit vector. The dot product combines two vectors into a single number that encodes both their lengths and the angle between them, which is why so much of machine learning, from similarity search to attention, reduces to dot products between vectors. Stack many vectors side by side and you get a matrix, and stack data into more than two dimensions and you get a tensor.

image.alt: A 3D Cartesian coordinate system with axes x, y, and z. A point in space is located by dropping to its x, y, and z coordinates, and the unit vectors i, j, and k mark the three coordinate directions at that point.
image.caption: A vector is an ordered list of numbers that names a point, or an arrow from the origin to it. Its coordinates are the amounts along each independent direction (here the unit vectors i, j, and k), so the same rules extend from this 3D picture to the thousand-dimensional vectors that encode data in machine learning.
